news.title: 🚨 Club World Cup: both finalists' training hit by weather ⛈️😱
news.lede: This World Cup weather, honestly... Argentina and Spain's preparations have been disrupted by their inability to train this Saturday. A thunderstorm led to the cancellation of both teams' training sessions, just over 24 hours before the World Cup final. The first to suffer were the Argentinians, who had to postpone their training session and go ride stationary bikes indoors.

news.title: Colombia and Argentina at the World Cup: Two Ways of Understanding Football and Life
news.lede: Colombia and Argentina have big differences. Before the tournament started, this World Cup gave Colombia the chance to vindicate the American dream of millions of Colombians who migrated to the United States searching for glory, yet the dream died early again. The group of players led by James Rodriguez, Luis Diaz, and Nestor Lorenzo promised they had prepared to stay as long as possible in the tournament. What was possible was five games.

news.title: World Cup: 'The Beautiful Game' in Miami had highs and lows
news.lede: MIAMI GARDENS — The party is ending. The memories will never fade away. Miami’s contribution to World Cup 2026 is in the books with seven matches before a packed Hard Rock Stadium. And we’re not talking only about the weather.

news.title: Spain cancels training before World Cup final; Argentina delayed by storms
news.lede: Editor's note: Follow for live updates on England-France World Cup game. MORRISTOWN, NJ — Both teams competing in the FIFA World Cup final saw their final training sessions before Sunday's match affected by thunderstorms rolling through the northern New Jersey area where Spain and Argentina were set to practice. Spain was scheduled to begin its session at 11 a.m. ET, and its bus arrived as scheduled to the facility.

news.title: 'Less than human': how unhoused people in Atlanta are being treated during the World Cup – video
news.lede: “This is what happens when you apply these incredibly violent economic forces to these cities … it's happened at every World Cup I've been to,” explains our chief sports writer, Barney Ronay. Employees in Atlanta, Georgia, recently threw away tents, medication, identification and other belongings of unhoused people at a public park without warning. This led activists and a local official to point to an apparent violation of procedures created after a city employee ran over a tent with a front loader last year, killing a man. Cornelius Taylor was crushed inside his home as workers came to clear a homeless encampment.

news.title: ‘Where did they go?’: homeless people feel force of America’s brutality in World Cup clean-up
news.lede: Fifa says football brings the world together but the unhoused in Atlanta feel targeted and completely excluded from the tournament. “A lot of our community has been pushed out by the World Cup. We’re not just dollar signs, we’re more than that. We’re people and we’re frustrated that they’ve chosen to treat us less than human.”

news.title: ‘Rifts began to appear’: Why many Latin Americans want Spain to beat Argentina in the World Cup final
news.lede: A history of racism and ongoing footballing rivalries have made the choice of which team to get behind in Sunday’s final a charged topic. The Brazilian journalist and columnist Julia Duailibi usually writes about politics in her weekly column for the leading Brazilian newspaper O Globo, but last Thursday she took a different tack, writing instead about why she would not be supporting neighbouring Argentina in the World Cup final. “I have always been an admirer of the hermanos [as Brazilians affectionately call Argentinians] and would have loved to cheer on a fellow South American team,” she wrote on the morning after Argentina’s dramatic semi-final victory over England. “But I admit that the racist scenes involving a minority of the fans, and the silence of the majority on the pitch, turned my stomach.”
